feed.title: Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants

entry.title: Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants
entry.summary:
In the analysis of sibling commitments within the Ark protocol, it appears that their benefits are largely limited. The primary scenario where sibling commitments could theoretically reduce costs involves the spending of a connector output, which is pre-created and designated for a specific forfeit transaction in case of an on-chain withdrawal. However, the realization of these cost savings necessitates the use of a specialized witness program, either Pay-to-Bare-Tapscript or Pay-to-TemplateHash, similar to bare CTV. Such configurations could potentially save approximately 16 virtual bytes (vb), but this advantage only materializes under the specific condition that an operator must publish the forfeit transaction.
Furthermore, the compatibility of these enhancements with existing systems is restricted. The discussion highlights that options like Josh * (1) and (2) align only with script-based vector commitments. This is due to the limitations posed by tapleaf commitments, which would otherwise induce a hash cycle. This technical constraint underscores the challenges in integrating sibling commitments seamlessly within the current framework of Ark, pointing to a need for careful consideration of system architectures when attempting to implement such features.
